coredata.dc:description: Tea residue is a representative biomass waste with a high annual output. Carbon dots (CDs) with antibacterial activity were developed from oolong tea waste to better develop high-value applications of tea residue. The prepared CDs possessed a graphite lattice structure and a negative charge (−17.4 mV). The CDs exhibited excellent antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, especially being highly effective against to Staphylococcus aureus, with a minimum inhibitory concentration of 0.125 mg/mL. The proposed antibacterial mechanism of CDs against S. aureus includes disrupting the bacterial cell membrane, leading to the leakage of contents and inducing oxidative stress. Additionally, CDs exhibited low resistance and cytotoxicity in drug resistance experiments and in vitro cytotoxicity tests. This study emphasizes the potential of developing antimicrobial materials from tea residue, thereby expanding the utilization pathways of biomass waste.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Oolong tea residue CDs. (A) UV–visible absorption spectra of carbon dots in water, inset is an image of pure water and carbon dots under UV irradiation, (B) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs with emission wavelengths from 290 to 400 nm, (C) Decay curves of the CDs detected at 350 nm in water, (D) TEM image (scale bar, 20 nm) of CDs, (E) XRD pattern, and (F) FT-IR spectrum of CDs.

Inactivation kinetics of S. aureus treated with CDs. (A) Growth curves of S. aureus treated with different concentrations of CDs. (B) Viability of S. aureus treated with CDs at various concentrations in a time-dependent manner (1, 2, 3,4,5,6 h) and measured by spread plate method.

Fluorescence images of S. aureus were processed in a time-dependent manner (0, 3, 6 h) with CDs (FITC filters were used to visualize green SYTO alone, TXRED filters were used to visualize propidium iodide alone). Scale bars, 20 nm.

Mechanistic study to understand the antibacterial properties of CDs. (A) Leakage of proteins after treatment with the CDs. (B) Effect of CDs on S. aureus cell wall. (C–D) SEM observations of bacterial cells, both untreated (Control) and treated with 1x MIC, at magnifications of 20,000x and 40,000x, respectively.

(A) Changes in ROS production in CDs-treated-resistant S. aureus at different time intervals (0,2,4,6 h) under fluorescence microscopy(a-d) ROS production in S. aureus after 6 h of PBS treatment(e). Scale bars, 20 nm. (B) Quantitative analysis of ROS generation in S. aureus treated with CDs was conducted using the DCFH-DA dye.
Monitoring of drug-resistant development of S.aureus after exposure to CDs and gentamicin at sub-MIC concentrations over 15 serial passages.
Cytotoxicity of CDs at different concentrations in LO2 cells. Different letters a and b indicate significant differences (P < 0.05).
Antimicrobial activity of CDs against foodborne pathogenic bacteria (mg/mL).
Antimicrobial activity of Chemically Derived CDs and Biomass-Derived CDs Against S. aureus.

head.ce:title: The reutilization of oolong tea waste: Synthesis of carbon dots and antibacterial mechanism against Staphylococcus aureus

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tea residue is a representative biomass waste with a high annual output. Carbon dots (CDs) with antibacterial activity were developed from oolong tea waste to better develop high-value applications of tea residue. The prepared CDs possessed a graphite lattice structure and a negative charge (−17.4 mV). The CDs exhibited excellent antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, especially being highly effective against to Staphylococcus aureus, with a minimum inhibitory concentration of 0.125 mg/mL. The proposed antibacterial mechanism of CDs against S. aureus includes disrupting the bacterial cell membrane, leading to the leakage of contents and inducing oxidative stress. Additionally, CDs exhibited low resistance and cytotoxicity in drug resistance experiments and in vitro cytotoxicity tests. This study emphasizes the potential of developing antimicrobial materials from tea residue, thereby expanding the utilization pathways of biomass waste.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the progress of society and the improvement of living standards, the public's pursuit of health has become increasingly intense, and the concept of antibacterial agents has gradually become deeply rooted in people's minds (Zhang et al., 2022). Antibacterial materials are functional materials that can reduce microbial activity and effectively kill and inhibit the growth and reproduction of microorganisms (Gao et al., 2023). Natural antibacterial materials are more popular due to their characteristics, such as abundant raw materials, low toxicity, high safety, and biodegradability(Osman et al., 2024). Numerous natural products have been isolated, including chitosan(Yang et al., 2023), antimicrobial peptides(Tao et al., 2025), plant polyphenols(Gao, Chen, et al., 2023), and essential oils (Tang et al., 2023), among others. Nonetheless, the practical application of these materials is significantly constrained by factors such as the instability of compounds during processing, technical challenges associated with large-scale production, and the high costs of traditional extraction methods (Socas et al., 2021). Consequently, developing new and powerful natural antibacterial materials is significant for society to meet the urgent demands of people for a secure and healthy lifestyle.

The ongoing advancement in nanotechnology has fostered many prospective antimicrobial nanomaterials capable of effectively managing and combating pathogenic microbes (Perumalsamy et al., 2024). For instance, metal nanomaterials including silver (Ag), copper (Cu), and zinc oxide (ZnO) have demonstrated remarkable antimicrobial properties. Nevertheless, the production cost of these materials is relatively high, and the utilization of toxic solvents during the manufacturing process may pose adverse impacts on the environment (Mohammed et al., 2024). Consequently, the application scope of these materials is somewhat restricted. Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging category of nanomaterials with distinctive attributes and boasting benefits such as minimal production costs, ease of surface modification, and enhanced biocompatibility compared to their nanomaterial counterparts (Zhang et al., 2023). Antimicrobial CD synthesis is divided into two categories based on the type of raw material used: chemical- and biomass-based. In contrast to chemical-based CDs, extracting CDs from renewable, affordable, and abundant biomass addresses the pressing need for the large-scale synthesis of natural nanomaterials while promoting sustainable development and carbon neutrality(Fang et al., 2024). Plant-based biomass is an appropriate starting material for CDs because these precursors contain various active substances, such as vitamins, amino acids, proteins, aromatic compounds, phenols, and essential oils(Miao et al., 2023). During the high-temperature process of CDs, these natural functional groups are inherently retained within the CDs(Zhao et al., 2023); therefore, excessive doping with chemical reagents on the surface of CDs is usually unnecessary.

Tea is the most widely consumed beverage globally, attributed to numerous health-promoting properties, such as cancer prevention, antimicrobial activity, cardiovascular disease mitigation, and hypoglycemic effects(Debnath et al., 2021). Tea residue, an ancillary product of tea beverage manufacture and tea leaf advanced processing, is predominantly (>90%) disposed of through incineration, with only a negligible fraction recycled, resulting in substantial squandering of biological resources(Akbayrak et al., 2020). However, it is worth noting that research on converting tea residue into value-added products is relatively limited(Duarah et al., 2024). Tea residues contain various bioactive compounds, including polyphenols (catechins, flavonoids, and proanthocyanidins), alkaloids (caffeine, theophylline, and theobromine), and terpenoid compounds(Wei et al., 2023). Consequently, tea residue, an inexpensive and abundant biomass resource, possesses immense potential for developing CDs endowed with a spectrum of bioactivities.

Oolong tea, indigenous to Fujian Province in China, is celebrated for its distinctive floral and fruity fragrance(Wang et al., 2022). Employing oolong tea residue for fabricating natural antimicrobial nanoparticles has been scarcely explored. This investigation employed oolong tea residue as a carbon precursor to synthesize antimicrobial CDs using an eco-friendly one-step hydrothermal synthesis. The synthesized CDs were thoroughly characterized, and their antimicrobial efficacy was evaluated. To decipher the underlying antibacterial mechanisms, this study examined the bacterial growth, morphological alterations, structural integrity of the bacterial cell wall and membrane, and increased reactive oxygen species (ROS) after CD treatment. Moreover, the study evaluated the antibiotic resistance and cytotoxicity profiles of the fabricated CDs, thereby paving the way for their prospective application in food safety and biotherapeutic interventions.

Oolong tea was procured from Wuyi Star Tea Industrial Co., Ltd., Fujian Province, China. Staphylococcus aureus (NBRC 100910), Bacillus cereus (ATCC 14579), Escherichia coli (ATCC 15224), and Proteus vulgaris (ATCC 13315), from the Key Laboratory of Microbiology and Enzyme Engineering of Fujian Province, Jimei University, Xiamen, China. Tryptic soy agar (TSA) and tryptic soy broth (TSB) were acquired from Huankai Microbial Technology Co. Ltd., Guangdong Province, China. SYTO 9, a nucleic acid stain for live cell imaging, and propidium iodide (PI) staining agent, along with a ROS assay kit, were supplied by Jiangsu Kaiji Biotechnology Co., Ltd. and Beyotime Biotechnology Research Institute, respectively. All chemical reagents used in the experimental procedures were of analytical purity and procured from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.

Oolong tea residue was used as a carbon precursor for fabricating CDs. Initially, 10 g of oolong tea was steeped in 200 mL of distilled water at 80 °C with agitation for 30 min to mimic the tea infusion process. Subsequently, the tea residue was separated by filtration. The resulting filtrate was freeze-dried to yield a concentrated tea powder, which was then stored at 4 °C for subsequent use (limited to a maximum of 12 weeks). The CDs synthesis involved transferring the tea residue into a Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor and processing it at 200 °C for 8 h. The post-treatment solution was naturally cooled to room temperature (26 ± 2 °C) under atmospheric conditions, followed by filtration to eliminate large particles. The filtrate was concentrated to 10–15 mL using a rotary evaporator. Then, the concentrate was centrifuged at 15,000×g for 15 min to separate the supernatant. This supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and dialyzed overnight against deionized water using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da. Finally, the dialysate was freeze-dried to obtain the CD powder.

The morphological characteristics of the CDs were determined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-2100, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). An X-ray diffractometer (XRD-6100, Rigaku Smartlab, Japan) was used to study crystallographic features. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was conducted using a Bruker ALPHA II spectrometer to analyze the surface functional groups of the CDs. Elemental analysis was performed using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific) to determine the CDs composition. The Zeta potentials were measured using Zetasizer 3000HSA (Malvern Instruments Ltd, Malvern, UK.). Fluorescence emission spectra, fluorescence lifetime, and quantum yield were obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-2700, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Furthermore, ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA)(Cui et al., 2024).

The minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) of CDs and their precursor freeze-dried tea powder against bacteria were determined using the two-fold dilution method(Lin et al., 2022). First, four bacterial strains, S aureus, B. cereus, E. coli, and P. vulgaris, were individually cultured overnight in TSB medium at 37 °C until they reached the logarithmic growth phase. Subsequently, antimicrobial activity was evaluated. Bacterial suspensions were diluted to 107 CFU/mL using TSB medium. Aseptic 96-well microplates were filled with 100 μL TSB. Then, the CDs and freeze-dried tea powder reconstituted in TSB were introduced into the initial well. The mixture was homogenized using a pipette, and 100 μL of the resultant solution was sequentially transferred to the following wells, facilitating a stepwise two-fold dilution (The concentrations of CDs and freeze-dried tea powder were 4, 2, 1, 0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.0625 mg/ml). In parallel, wells that consisted of the diluted bacterial suspension and TSB were designated as the positive controls, whereas wells containing the medium alone were used as the negative controls. The 96-well plate was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and the turbidity of each well was checked visually. MIC is determined as the lowest concentration at which no bacterial growth was observed. Each concentration was assayed in triplicate to ensure reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth curve and time-killing kinetics determination
ce:section.ce:para: According to prior research(Cui et al., 2023), the bactericidal and bacteriostatic profiles of the CDs were evaluated. The bacterial cultures were incubated in TSB at 37 °C for 24 h and diluted to a final concentration of 107 CFU/mL. The CDs were introduced into the bacterial suspensions in each test tube to attain final concentrations of 1/2 × , 1 × , 2 × , and 4 × the established MIC. The prepared cultures, each aliquoted into a volume of 200 μL, were transferred to a 96-well microplate. Microbial cell growth was monitored by measuring the optical density (OD) at 600 nm using a microbial growth curve analyzer (Shanghai Xinzhi Bio-Technology Co., Ltd., Model MGC-200).

The time-kill experiment was executed per the protocol established by Yang et al.(Yang et al., 2020), with minor adaptations. Bacterial suspensions at a concentration of 107 CFU/mL were subjected to CDs at multiples of the MIC, specifically 1 × , 2 × , and 4 × MIC. A culture devoid of CDs was used as the negative control, while TSB lacking CDs served as the blank control. Following incubation at 37 °C for designated intervals (0, 2, 4, and 6 h), 100 μL aliquots of each bacterial suspension were evenly spread onto TSA plates. Subsequently, the plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h to facilitate colony enumeration. The experiment was conducted in triplicate to ensure the reliability of the results.

SEM was utilized to examine bacterial morphology based on previous method(Lu et al., 2024). The prepared CDs were added into the bacterial suspension of 107 CFU/mL at the final concentration of 1 × MIC, with a control group consisting of a bacterial solution without CDs. This mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 6 h. The bacterial cells were collected by centrifugation at 5000×g for 15 min, and fixed with a 2.5% glutaraldehyde solution at 4 °C for 12 h. The samples then underwent a gradient dehydration process using ethanol at concentrations of 30%, 50%, 70%, 85%, 95%, and 100%, with a 10 min application for each concentration. The bacterial suspension in 100% ethanol was placed onto a coverslip and allowed to evaporate naturally. Finally, the coverslip with the bacterial was attached to the specimen stage with carbon conductive adhesive, sputter-coated with gold, and then positioned into the SEM for observation.

SYTO 9/PI staining was used to investigate the ability of the CDs to disrupt bacterial membranes. Bacterial suspensions of 107 CFU/mL were treated with CDs to achieve a final concentration of 1 × MIC and incubated at 37 °C for 6 h, with a control group that did not receive CDs. Use 70% isopropanol as a positive control.After incubating for various durations (0, 3, 6 h) at 37 °C, the samples were centrifuged at 4 °C at 10,000×g for 10 min and then resuspended in PBS. Next, 500 μL of the bacterial suspension was mixed with 3 μL each of SYTO 9 and PI. The mixture was incubated in the dark for 15 min. Finally, 5 μL of the incubated bacterial suspension was applied to a microscope slide, and the fluorescent images of the bacterial samples were recorded using a fluorescence microscope.(Cui et al., 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protein and alkaline phosphatase (AKP) leakage assay
ce:section.ce:para: In alignment with methodologies reported in a preceding publication(Zhang et al., 2024), the BCA protein assay kit was employed to evaluate the influence of CDs on extracellular protein levels in S. aureus. CDs were added to bacterial suspensions at a concentration of 107 CFU/mL to achieve final concentrations of 1 × and 2 × MIC, and then incubated at 37 °C for 0, 2, 4, and 6 h, with a control group consisting of a bacterial solution without CDs. The protein content in the supernatant was quantitatively analyzed using the BCA protein assay kit. Concurrently, changes in AKP content were recorded following the protocol outlined in the AKP activity assay kit.

Intracellular ROS production in CDs-treated bacteria was assayed using a slightly modified method by Cui et al.(Cui et al., 2024). CDs were added to bacterial suspensions at a concentration of 107 CFU/mL to achieve a final concentration of 1 × MIC, and then incubated at 37 °C for 0, 2, 4, and 6 h, with a control group consisting of a bacterial solution without CDs. The bacterial cells were centrifuged at 8000×g for 10 min and resuspended in equal PBS. Briefly, 10 μL of 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA, 10 mmol/L) was added to the suspension. After incubating the suspension at 37 °C for 30 min, the cells were centrifuged, resuspended, and washed three times to remove excess fluorescent probes. Untreated bacteria served as controls. Fluorescence images of the bacterial samples were recorded using a fluorescence microscope. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of the bacterial suspensions was measured at excitation and emission wavelengths of 488 nm and 525 nm, respectively.

Drug resistance was assessed by repeated treatment of S. aureus with antimicrobial agents(Chen et al., 2019). To compare with traditional antibiotics, we selected an antibiotic, gentamicin, for comparison. First, the initial MIC of gentamicin and CDs against S. aureus was obtained through the broth dilution experiment. Then, the bacteria were exposed to sub-MIC (1/4 of the MIC at the previous passage for the same antimicrobial agents) and cultured at 37 °Cto the logarithmic phase. The bacteria obtained from the culture were prepared in saline to make a new bacterial dilution 1.0 × 107 CFU/ml and used for the next MIC detection. These steps were repeated and the MIC values from the first passage to the fifteenth passage were recorded. The drug resistance behavior of the bacteria was demonstrated by observing the change in the ratio of the MIC to that of the first passage.

The 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of CDs on human normal liver cells LO2 (Cui et al., 2022). The cells in the logarithmic growth phase were harvested. The cell suspension was adjusted to a concentration of 6 × 104 cells/mL and inoculated into a 96-well plate. After 80% of the cells had adhered, the plate was washed with PBS. The experimental groups were supplemented with varying concentrations of CDs (0.125, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, and 4 mg/mL) and DMEM, whereas the control group did not receive the CDs. The blank group contained neither cells nor the CDs. The 96-well plates were incubated overnight in a humidified atmosphere at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Afterwards, the medium was removed, and MTT solution (5 mg/mL, 50 μL) was added to each well to measure the effect of different concentrations of CDs on cell viability. After 4 h of incubation, the supernatant was discarded, and 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide solution was added. The cell viability was calculated by measuring the absorbance of the mixed solution at 570 nm using a multifunctional microplate reader (Varioskan LUX, Thermo Fisher, USA)(Guo et al., 2024).

The experimental results were analyzed using the Statistical Package for the Social Sciences software (version 22.0), and the significance of the differences was tested using a single-factor analysis of variance, with P < 0.05 indicating significant differences.

In this study, CDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal synthesis using oolong tea dregs as a carbon source. The UV absorption spectra and fluorescence properties were investigated. Fig. 1 A illustrates that the as-synthesized CDs were disrupted homogenously in an aqueous medium and emitted blue fluorescence upon excitation at 365 nm under ultraviolet illumination. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum disclosed typical absorption bands at 214 and 270 nm, corresponding to the π-π∗ electronic transitions of aromatic C=C sp2 bonds and the n-π∗ conjugated transitions of C=O groups, respectively(Wen et al., 2023). Fig. 1B presents the fluorescence spectrum of the CDs. The fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs in aqueous solution were measured at different excitation wavelengths. As the excitation wavelength was increased from 290 to 400 nm, the maximum emission wavelength shifted, and the fluorescence intensity initially increased and then gradually decreased. The CDs exhibited a maximum excitation wavelength of 350 nm and a maximum emission wavelength of 420 nm. Variations in the excitation wavelength cause shifts in the emission peak and corresponding changes in intensity (De et al., 2013). Fig. 1C presents the fluorescence decay curve, fitted with a bi-exponential function, yielding a fluorescence lifetime of 6.71 ns for the CDs. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the quantum yield (QY) of the CDs was calculated to be 8.2%. These results display that the CDs have tunable fluorescence and reasonable luminescence lifetimes, consistent with the optical characteristics of biomass fluorescent CDs(Fang et al., 2024).

Fig. 1D illustrates the morphological characteristics and particle size distribution of the CDs, demonstrating that the CDs exhibit a spherical morphology with a homogeneous dispersion. The average diameter of the CDs was 2.04 ± 0.57 nm. The polydispersity index (PDI) assesses the uniformity and stability of CDs. A higher PDI value indicates a wider range of particle sizes, whereas a lower PDI value signifies a more consistent size distribution(Mondéjar et al., 2024). The PDI value obtained was 0.307, indicating uniform dispersion of the CDs within the suspension. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the CDs shows a broad diffraction peak at an approximate at 2θ = 21.79°(Fig. 1E), corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite carbon(Zhao, Zhang, Wang, & Devahastin, 2020).

The physicochemical properties of the CDs were further investigated using FT-IR spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to examine their surface functionalities. Fig. 1F illustrates that the pronounced absorption band at 3380 cm−1 is ascribed to the O-H stretching vibrations associated with hydrogen bonding, indicating the enrichment of hydroxyl (-OH) groups on the surface of the CDs(Lai et al., 2021). The characteristic peak at 2960 cm−1 is indicative of C-H stretching vibrations within aromatic rings, while the peaks at 1656 and 1440 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the carbonyl (C=O) and ether (C-O) groups, respectively (Wang et al., 2020). The XPS analysis (Fig. S1) revealed the elemental composition of the CDs, with carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N) present in relative atomic percentages of 70.38%, 22.49%, and 7.14%, respectively. The Zeta potential measured at −15.4 ± 1.21 mV signifies a substantial negative charge on the CDs surface, conforming to an abundance of negatively charged oxygen-containing functional groups, such as -OH, C-O, and C=O, over positively charged groups. These findings suggest that the CDs inherited bioactive functional groups from the tea leaves, encompassing -OH, C=C, C-O, and C=O groups (Gao et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative analysis results of MICs of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antimicrobial activity of the CDs against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria commonly encountered in several food materials was evaluated, and the results are presented in Table 1 . The CDs exhibited antimicrobial effects against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, with the most pronounced effect observed against S. aureus, which exhibited an MIC of 0.125 mg/mL. In contrast, antimicrobial activity against Bacillus cereus was relatively weak, with an MIC of 1 mg/mL. The MIC for the Gram-negative bacteria Escherichia coli and Proteus vulgaris were 2 and 0.5 mg/mL, respectively. The varying antimicrobial activities of the CDs could be due to differences in the bacterial cell wall structures, possibly influencing their interactions with the CDs(Gao et al., 2022). In light of these observations, S. aureus, exhibiting heightened sensitivity to the CDs, was chosen as the model bacterium for subsequent investigations. The antimicrobial activity of CDs is closely related to their precursor substances because CDs can retain some of the properties of their precursors(Li et al., 2022). For example, Wu et al.(Wu et al., 2022) used a simple hydrothermal synthesis to synthesize CDs with good antimicrobial properties by retaining the active moiety of levofloxacin with a doubling of the original inhibitory effect ratio enhancement. Ma et al.(Ma et al., 2020) reported that CDs prepared from cinnamon leaves could inhibit >85% of S. aureus and E. coli at a MIC of 1000 μg/mL. Lu et al. successfully prepared curcumin carbon dots using a hydrothermal synthesis method with curcumin and citric acid as precursors. These synthesized carbon dots not only improved the water solubility and stability of curcumin but also exhibited good antimicrobial properties, with an MIC of 375 μg/mL against S. aureus(Lu et al., 2021). As shown in Table 2 , chemically derived CDs typically exhibit superior antibacterial efficacy compared to biomass-derived CDs. However, the use of chemical precursors is associated with higher costs. Conversely, pharmaceutically active biomass by-products from various fields can serve as cost-effective precursors for synthesizing CDs, thereby promoting environmental protection and resource recycling. Notably, CDs derived from oolong tea residues demonstrate enhanced antibacterial activity against S. aureus than most biomass-derived CDs. A comparative assessment was conducted between the bactericidal effects of freeze-dried tea powder and CDs on S. aureus to further delineate the bactericidal capabilities of CDs. After 24 h of co-incubation, the medium of the control group was replete with bacterial colonies, whereas a noticeable decrease in colony count was observed in both the freeze-dried tea powder and CDs treatments (Fig. S2). After CDs treatment, only a small number of colonies were present on the plates at a concentration of 0.125 mg/mL, which is the MIC of CDs. However, more colonies were observed in the freeze-dried tea powder group. When the concentration was increased to 0.25 mg/mL, no colonies were observed in the CDs group. The inhibitory effect of CDs on S. aureus was concentration-dependent and was better than that of the freeze-dried tea powder. Tea extracts exhibited a favorable inhibitory effect on S. aureus, consistent with the findings of other studies(Zhao et al., 2022). Comprehensive analyses indicated that the preparation of CDs using tea dregs inherited bioactive components and demonstrated significant antimicrobial properties due to their unique nanostructure.

Subsequently, the bacteriostatic and bactericidal kinetics of different CDs concentrations were studied. The growth curves of the bacteria at different concentrations of CDs were assessed by measuring the OD of the cultures at 600 nm (Fig. 2 A). After 5 h of incubation, untreated S. aureus entered the logarithmic growth phase, exhibited a rapid growth trend within 10 h, and entered the plateau phase. The growth of S. aureus treated with different concentrations of CDs was inhibited. Treatment with 0.5 × MIC delayed the start of the logarithmic growth phase by 2 h. In contrast, treatment with 1 × and 2 × MIC completely inhibited the growth of S. aureus.

To assess bactericidal effects (Fig. 2B), the bacterial counts of S. aureus in the control group increased over the 6 h period, whereas the bacterial counts did not increase after treatment with different concentrations of CDs. After treatment with the MIC and higher concentrations of CDs, the number of bacteria gradually decreased with increasing treatment time. The number of S. aureus decreased from 4.79 to 3.01 log after 6 h of treatment with 1 × MIC concentration of CDs, while 2 × MIC concentration of CDs produced a 100% bactericidal effect. The combined bactericidal and bacteriostatic curves indicate that the CDs exhibit strong concentration- and time-dependent antibacterial effects against S. aureus at concentrations above 0.125 mg/mL. Lang et al. prepared CDs using rutin as a carbon source, presenting excellent antimicrobial efficacy against S. aureus within 8 h(Lang et al., 2024). Similarly, Lin et al. explored the effect of onion-derived CDs against Pseudomonas aeruginosa and achieved complete bacterial killing within 6 h at a MIC of 2 mg/mL(Lin et al., 2022). Comprehensive analyses revealed that CDs prepared using tea dregs were comparable to other biomass-derived CDs regarding antimicrobial efficiency, demonstrating significant bactericidal capacity. This highlights the potential application of tea dregs as a sustainable resource for preparing efficient antimicrobial materials and confirms the potential of biomass-derived CDs for numerous antimicrobial applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of bacterial live/dead fluorescence staining
ce:section.ce:para: After CDs treatment, cell membrane integrity in S. aureus was assessed using the fluorescent probes SYTO 9 and PI under fluorescent microscopy. The nucleic acid stain PI selectively stains the DNA of bacteria with disrupted cell membranes, whereas SYTO 9 permeates and stains the DNA of all bacteria, rendering the SYTO 9/PI dual-staining technique a credible approach to evaluate bacterial cell membrane integrity(Pan et al., 2022). Both CDs-treated and control groups were stained with SYTO 9 and displayed a high level of green fluorescence (Fig. 3 ). The absence of PI staining in the control group suggested that most bacterial cell membranes were not damaged. After 3 h of CDs exposure, a significant increase in bacteria was observed with PI staining, indicating varying degrees of cell membrane damage. 70% isopropanol, as a commonly used disinfectant and bactericide, is often used as a positive control to assess the bacterial membrane damage caused by antimicrobial agents. It can be observed that the membrane damage effect after 6 h of treatment with carbon dots is similar to that of the positive control group treated with 70% isopropanol. A strong red fluorescence signal was observed in the PI channel, while the fluorescence signal of SYTO 9 was weak, indicating that the cell membranes of most bacteria were severely damaged, allowing PI to enter the bacteria and compete with SYTO 9 for binding sites. Gagic et al. indicated that amine-modified CDs had a significant antimicrobial effect on S. aureus. After 2 × MIC CDs treatment, S. aureus was almost eliminated due to disruption of cell membrane integrity. Therefore, S. aureus was almost undetectable in the SYTO 9 channel. In contrast, after 1 × MIC CDs treatment, most S. aureus cells were killed, and a weak SYTO 9 fluorescence signal was observed(Gagic et al., 2020). The results revealed that oolong tea-derived CDs caused cell membrane damage in S. aureus and eliminated most bacterial populations within 6 h, consistent with bactericidal kinetic data.

The proficiency of CDs to lyse cellular membranes was manifested by extruding intracellular constituents, including proteins and AKP(Wu et al., 2023). Fig. 4 A delineates the concentration of extracellular proteins in S. aureus following CDs treatment. A marked escalation in the level of extracellular soluble proteins was observed after 4 h of incubation with CDs. After 6 h of CDs exposure at 1 × and 2 × MIC, the extracellular soluble protein concentrations ascended to peak values of 76.84 and 81.05 μg/mL, respectively. AKP is an enzyme of considerable importance in microbial systems(Kang et al., 2020). Since AKP is released only when the cell wall is compromised, extracellular AKP activity can be used to assess the extent of cell wall damage. Extracellular AKP activity in the control group remained unchanged after 6 h. In contrast, extracellular AKP activity increased progressively with the duration of CDs treatment. The AKP activity reached a plateau after 6 h of exposure. After 6 h of treatment with 1 × and 2 × MIC of CDs, the extracellular AKP activity increased by 6.6- and 7.9-fold compared to the control group, respectively (Fig. 4B). These results suggest that CDs disrupt microbial cell wall/membrane integrity, releasing proteins and important enzymes from the cell, ultimately leading to bacterial death. Wang et al.(Wang et al., 2023) prepared CDs from garlic, severely hindering ion exchange and substance transport inside and outside the cell by interfering with the normal function of ion channels in the cell membrane. Over time, the cell wall and membrane are irreversibly damaged. Protein concentration and AKP activity in the bacterial solution increased significantly within 120 min in the 200 μg/mL CDs-treated group. The protein concentration was approximately 100 μg/mL at 20 μg/mL. AKP activity was also significantly increased to approximately seven times that of the control group. This phenomenon is similar to the results of the experiments described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on the morphology of S. aureus
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of the CDs on the microstructure of the bacterial cells was examined using SEM. The SEM images demonstrated that the bacterial cells in the untreated group possessed distinct edges and a uniformly smooth cell membrane surface (Fig. 4C). In stark contrast, a 6 h exposure to CDs induced discernible alterations on the bacterial surfaces. The images depicted pronounced wrinkles and frank cell membrane disruption (Fig. 4D). These observations suggest that CDs exert their effects on the bacterial surface, precipitating profound morphological alterations indicative of cellular damage. Catechins in tea play a crucial role as core components of its antibacterial activity. These bioactive molecules can interact closely with bacterial cell walls and membranes, disrupt normal metabolic mechanisms, and effectively inhibit or destroy them(Ignasimuthu et al., 2019). Tea-dreg-derived CDs may retain these valuable active components during their preparation(Li et al., 2021).Similar results were reported by Li et al., who investigated the effect of CDs on S. aureus and concluded that CDs with oxygen-containing groups (C-O and C=O) were absorbed by the bacterial cell wall, thus preventing nutrient ingress and bacterial reproduction(Li et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of bacterial intracellular ROS levels
ce:section.ce:para: After treatment with CDs, the ROS levels were measured using the fluorescent indicator DCFH-DA to investigate the mechanism of CD damage in bacterial cells. ROS are natural byproducts of normal cellular oxygen metabolism; however, excessive ROS accumulation within bacterial cells can destroy cellular membranes, DNA, and proteins, resulting in bacterial death(Yu et al., 2022). Fig. 5 A illustrates the ROS levels generated in bacteria following treatment with varying concentrations of CDs. The control group, devoid of ROS induction, exhibited a comparatively dark field of view under a microscope. In contrast, bacteria treated with CDs for 2 h demonstrated faint green fluorescence, indicating ROS production initiated by CDs. After 6 h of treatment with the CDs, the fluorescence signal intensity peaked, characterized by a robust green emission (Fig. 5B). Notably, no green fluorescence was observed in the group incubated with PBS for 6 h, confirming the specificity of the ROS response to the CDs treatment. These findings suggest that CDs can penetrate bacterial cells and stimulate ROS overproduction, with fluorescence intensity escalating in tandem with the duration of CDs exposure. In a pivotal study, Li et al. elucidated the mechanism underlying how CDs, once internalized by bacteria, can precipitate metabolic disarray(Li, Han, et al., 2020). This disruption was attributed to the ability of CDs to perturb the equilibrium of oxidative enzymes, culminating in excessive intracellular ROS aggregation. The ensuing oxidative stress exerts a detrimental impact on cellular components, tipping the balance toward bacterial death. It has been reported that C=O is a more active site for ROS generation(Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023). The UV absorption spectra and FT-IR results confirmed the presence of a large amount of C=O in the CDs, possibly inducing ROS production and S. aureus cell death.

Gentamicin was used as a control to evaluate whether tea residue CDs could gradually induce bacterial resistance. In this experiment, the initial MIC of gentamicin against S. aureus was 15 μg/mL. S. aureus was repeatedly cultured at sub-lethal concentrations. Fig. 6 depicts the MIC of gentamicin against S. aureus increased by 15 times during the ninth generation, indicating that the bacteria developed resistance to this traditional antibiotic. As S. aureus was cultured at sub-lethal concentrations up to the 12th generation, the MIC of gentamicin doubled to 30 times its original value, and the experiment continued until the 15th generation, with the MIC slowly rising. After 15 treatment cycles, the MIC of CDs against S. aureus did not increase, indicating the absence of resistance development. Li et al.(Li, Liu, et al., 2020) prepared CDs using gentamicin and demonstrated their superior antibacterial ability and lower risk of drug resistance. It is unique in that CDs contain a cation-destroying mechanism and can induce ROS production inside the bacteria, thereby effectively destroying the bacterial structure and inhibiting its growth. CQDs bind less specifically to bacteria than antibiotics do, significantly reducing the likelihood of bacterial resistance. Similarly, oolong tea dreg-derived CDs can interact with bacterial cell walls and membranes and penetrate cell membranes and deep into the bacteria to generate excessive ROS that can potentially damage the bacteria. This multi-interaction mechanism enhances the bactericidal effect of CDs and greatly reduces the chances of bacterial development of drug resistance. These results suggest that CDs have an advantage over traditional antibiotics regarding drug resistance, and these novel antimicrobial CDs may help limit the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria.

The cytotoxicity of the CDs was evaluated using the MTT assay with normal human hepatocyte LO2. In this study, CDs at concentrations of 0.125, 0.25, 0.5, 1, and 2 mg/mL did not significantly inhibit cell growth. When the CDs concentration was increased to 4 mg/mL, only a weak inhibition (viability >85%) was observed (Fig. 7 ). This is consistent with other reports stating that HEK-293 cell viability remained >95% after treatment with radish residue-derived CDs, with no significant difference compared to the control group(Wang et al., 2024). Generally, CDs prepared from biomass as precursors are virtually non-toxic(Zeng et al., 2024). CDs toxicity in mammalian cells differs significantly from that in bacteria, possibly due to various factors, including the surface charge, functional groups, and size and shape of the CDs(Koutamehr et al., 2023). Mammalian cells may be more resistant to the adsorption and penetration of CDs due to their larger size and more complex cell membrane structure, thus reducing their potential impact(Min et al., 2022). Particularly, some CDs can generate ROS, which are essential for their antimicrobial activity. However, mammalian cells are frequently equipped with a more efficient antioxidant defense system, allowing them to effectively neutralize ROS and reduce the damage caused by oxidative stress(Ferreira et al., 2023). For example, some researchers triggered oxidative stress in bacteria at a concentration of 16 μg/mL using CDs prepared with spermidine as a carbon source. In contrast, after treatment of L929 cells with CDs up to 128 μg/mL for 24 h, the cell viability remained >95%(Cui et al., 2024). This finding highlights that CDs inhibit bacteria at low concentrations but exhibit good biocompatibility with mammalian cells at relatively high concentrations. These results indicate that CDs are effective and safe antimicrobial agents.

We synthesized CDs from oolong tea residue using a green, one-step hydrothermal process, yielding CDs with high water solubility, ultra-small size, rich surface functional groups, and a spherical structure. These CDs demonstrated potent antimicrobial activity against several foodborne pathogens, with notable efficacy against S. aureus (MIC = 0.125 mg/mL) and a low tendency to induce drug resistance. Post-treatment with the synthesized CDs, the integrity of the cell walls and membranes of S. aureus was compromised, leading to leakage of proteins and AKP. Additionally, CDs triggered oxidative stress and caused morphological damage to the bacteria. The CDs displayed exceedingly low toxicity to LO2 cells to human cells, highlighting their biosafety. Their antimicrobial properties, combined with their stability and low toxicity, make these CDs promising candidates for application in antimicrobial materials.

Weian Ye: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Shen Yang: Supervision, Formal analysis. Ritian Jin: Visualization. Duo Liang: Methodology. Zhongyang Ren: Validation. Rong Lin: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
